questions.prompt: Rigid motion that slides every point of a figure the same distance in the same direction.
questions.explanation: A translation maps every point (x,y) to (x+a,y+b), sliding the figure with no turning or flipping. Distances and angle measures are fully preserved, so the image is congruent to the preimage. NY G-CO.1 defines translations using vectors. Regents items often give the rule as T(x,y)=(x+a,y+b) and ask you to locate the image or identify the vector.

questions.prompt: Rigid motion that turns a figure about a fixed center through a given angle.
questions.explanation: A rotation turns every point of a figure the same angle about a fixed center. Common Regents rotations about the origin: R90(x,y)=(−y,x); R180(x,y)=(−x,−y); R270(x,y)=(y,−x). All distances and angles are preserved (G-CO.4). A 180° rotation is equivalent to point symmetry and maps every point through the center to the opposite side.

questions.prompt: Rigid motion that flips a figure across a line so the line is its perpendicular bisector for each point.
questions.explanation: A reflection flips a figure across a line so every point and its image are equidistant from that line. Common Regents lines: x-axis gives (x,−y); y-axis gives (−x,y); y=x gives (y,x). The reflection is an isometry — all distances and angle measures are preserved. G-CO.4. The line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector of every segment joining a point to its image.

questions.prompt: Composition of a reflection followed by a translation parallel to the line of reflection.
questions.explanation: A glide reflection is the composition of a reflection across a line followed by a translation parallel to that same line. It is the only indirect isometry among the four rigid motions, reversing orientation. Footprint patterns exhibit glide-reflection symmetry. G-CO.5. Because it reverses orientation, no single reflection or rotation can produce the same result.

questions.prompt: General name for any transformation that preserves both distance and angle measure.
questions.explanation: An isometry (Greek: isos=equal, metron=measure) preserves all distances and angle measures. Direct isometries (translations and rotations) keep orientation; indirect isometries (reflections and glide reflections) reverse it. G-CO.6. Felix Klein's 1872 Erlangen Program classified geometries by their isometry groups, unifying Euclidean geometry under a single framework.

questions.prompt: Notation such as (x,y) -> (x+3,y-2) used on Regents items to describe a transformation rule.
questions.explanation: Coordinate notation expresses a transformation as an algebraic rule, e.g. (x,y)→(x+3,y−2) for a translation or (x,y)→(−y,x) for a 90° counterclockwise rotation. G-CO.2 requires students to represent transformations in the plane using function notation. NY Regents items frequently ask you to identify or apply these rules from a table or diagram.

questions.prompt: Original figure before a transformation is applied.
questions.explanation: The preimage is the original figure before any transformation is applied. Its vertices are labeled with standard letters (A,B,C). After the transformation the resulting figure — the image — uses primed labels (A′,B′,C′). G-CO.2. On Regents items you are often given the preimage coordinates and must apply the transformation rule to find the image.

questions.prompt: Resulting figure after a transformation, usually labeled with primes such as A'.
questions.explanation: The image is the figure produced after a transformation is applied to the preimage. Vertices use prime notation (A′,B′,C′). A rigid-motion image is congruent to the preimage; a dilation image is similar. G-CO.2. Congruence or similarity can be proved by identifying the transformation that maps one figure onto the other and citing the appropriate theorem.

questions.prompt: Fixed point about which every point of a figure turns the same angle measure.
questions.explanation: The center of rotation is the fixed point about which all other points rotate. When the center is the origin, standard rules apply: R90(x,y)=(−y,x). When the center is another point P, translate P to the origin, rotate, then translate back. G-CO.4. On Regents items the center is often the origin or a labeled point on the figure itself.

questions.prompt: Line across which each point of a figure is mapped to its mirror image at equal distance.
questions.explanation: The line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector of every segment joining a preimage point to its image. Common Regents lines: x=0 gives (−x,y); y=0 gives (x,−y); y=x gives (y,x); y=−x gives (−y,−x). G-CO.4. To verify a line of reflection, confirm it is equidistant from and perpendicular to the segment joining each point and its image.

questions.prompt: Non-rigid transformation that produces a similar figure by stretching or shrinking from a center.
questions.explanation: A dilation produces a figure similar to the original by multiplying each distance from the center of dilation by scale factor k. Unlike rigid motions, dilation changes size but preserves shape and all angle measures. G-SRT.1 establishes dilation as the geometric foundation of similarity. Image vertices use prime notation; all corresponding sides are in ratio k:1.

questions.prompt: Ratio of image length to preimage length under a dilation.
questions.explanation: The scale factor k is the ratio of any image length to its corresponding preimage length: k = image side ÷ preimage side. If k=2, all lengths double; if k=0.5, all lengths halve. k>1 produces an enlargement; 0<k<1 a reduction. On the Regents, divide corresponding side lengths to find k. G-SRT.1a. Area scales by k², so doubling sides quadruples area.

questions.prompt: Fixed point from which a figure is enlarged or reduced under a dilation.
questions.explanation: The center of dilation is the fixed point from which all distances scale by factor k. With center at the origin: (x,y)→(kx,ky). Points on a line through the center stay on that line. G-SRT.1a. To locate the center: draw lines through each pair of corresponding vertices — they intersect at the center of dilation. Regents items may ask you to find or verify the center.

questions.prompt: Dilation with a scale factor greater than 1.
questions.explanation: An enlargement has scale factor k>1, so the image is larger than the preimage. Corresponding angles remain congruent; all side lengths multiply by k. Area scales by k². G-SRT.1. On Regents items, identify an enlargement by checking k = image length ÷ preimage length > 1, or by noting that the image is outside the preimage when the center is inside.

questions.prompt: Dilation with a scale factor between 0 and 1.
questions.explanation: A reduction has scale factor 0<k<1, so the image is smaller than the preimage. Corresponding angles are congruent; side lengths shrink by factor k. Area scales by k². G-SRT.1. Example: k=½ halves every side and cuts area to one-quarter. G-SRT.1b guarantees that a dilation maps any line segment not through the center to a parallel segment.

questions.prompt: Property of a figure that maps onto itself when reflected across a line.
questions.explanation: A figure has line symmetry (reflective symmetry) if it maps onto itself under reflection across at least one line. An isosceles triangle has 1 line of symmetry; a rectangle has 2; a regular hexagon has 6. G-CO.3. Regents items ask you to count lines of symmetry, identify them by equation, or explain why a given quadrilateral does or does not have line symmetry.

questions.prompt: Property of a figure that maps onto itself after a rotation of less than 360 degrees about its center.
questions.explanation: A figure has rotational symmetry if it maps onto itself after a rotation of less than 360°. The order equals the number of such rotations in one full turn. A regular n-gon has rotational symmetry of order n (rotate by 360°/n). G-CO.3. A square has order-4 rotational symmetry with 90°, 180°, and 270° rotations each mapping the square onto itself.

questions.prompt: Rotational symmetry of order 2 — every 180-degree rotation maps the figure onto itself.
questions.explanation: Point symmetry is rotational symmetry of order 2: a 180° rotation maps the figure onto itself. Equivalently, for every point P on the figure there exists a point P′ so the center is the midpoint of PP′. All parallelograms have point symmetry; isosceles triangles (without being equilateral) do not. G-CO.3. A circle has infinitely many rotational symmetries.

questions.prompt: Polygon with all sides congruent and all angles congruent — has both line and rotational symmetry.
questions.explanation: A regular polygon has all sides congruent and all interior angles congruent. A regular n-gon has n lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order n. Interior angle = (n−2)·180°/n. Euclid constructed the regular triangle, square, and pentagon in Book IV of the Elements (~300 BCE). G-CO.3 and G-CO.13 cover regular polygon symmetry and construction.

questions.prompt: Direction of the labeled vertices (clockwise or counterclockwise) — preserved by translation and rotation but reversed by reflection.
questions.explanation: Orientation describes the order (clockwise or counterclockwise) of labeled vertices of a figure. Direct isometries (translations, rotations) preserve orientation; indirect isometries (reflections, glide reflections) reverse it. G-CO.6. If two figures have opposite orientations, no direct isometry maps one to the other — a reflection or glide reflection is required.

questions.prompt: Result of performing one transformation followed by another, applied to the image of the first.
questions.explanation: A composition applies two or more transformations in sequence. Two reflections across parallel lines produce a translation; across intersecting lines, a rotation. G-CO.5. Regents items ask you to describe the composition (e.g., 'reflect across x-axis, then translate') that maps one figure onto another, or to apply two rules in sequence to find the final image.

questions.prompt: Pairs of vertices, one on the preimage and one on the image, that are matched by a transformation.
questions.explanation: Corresponding vertices are the matched pairs — A↔A′, B↔B′, C↔C′ — defined by the transformation. Congruence and similarity statements must list vertices in correct correspondence order (△ABC≅△DEF means A↔D, B↔E, C↔F). G-CO.7. Listing vertices in wrong order is a common Regents error that invalidates both the statement and subsequent CPCTC reasoning.

questions.prompt: Property of every rigid motion — segment lengths in the preimage equal those in the image.
questions.explanation: Distance preservation means every rigid motion keeps the length of every segment unchanged — preimage segment equals image segment in measure. This is the key property that makes rigid motions produce congruent figures. G-CO.6 defines congruence via rigid motions: two figures are congruent if and only if one maps to the other by a composition of rigid motions.

questions.prompt: Property of every rigid motion AND every dilation — angle measures stay the same.
questions.explanation: Angle preservation means both rigid motions and dilations keep all angle measures unchanged. Rigid motions preserve both distance and angle, producing congruence. Dilations preserve angle but scale distance, producing similarity. G-SRT.1b. This is why corresponding angles of similar figures are always congruent even when side lengths differ by the scale factor k.

questions.prompt: Statement such as Triangle ABC maps onto Triangle DEF that names the correspondence between preimage and image vertices.
questions.explanation: A mapping statement such as '△ABC maps onto △DEF under a 90° rotation about the origin' establishes the transformation, identifies vertex correspondence, and implies all six congruences (CPCTC) if the transformation is a rigid motion. G-CO.6. Writing a precise mapping statement is a common Regents constructed-response task worth significant partial credit.

questions.prompt: Statement at the top of a two-column proof that lists what may be assumed true at the start.
questions.explanation: The 'Given' section of a two-column proof lists all information provided in the problem — congruence marks, parallel lines, bisectors, etc. These are the starting facts from which all subsequent statements follow. G-CO.9/10. Correctly writing the given earns the first line of credit on Regents proofs; omitting it or adding information not in the diagram is a common scoring error.

questions.prompt: Statement that names what a two-column proof must establish from the given information.
questions.explanation: The 'Prove' statement declares the exact conclusion a two-column proof must reach, such as 'Prove: △ABC≅△DEF.' Every step must logically advance toward this conclusion, and the final statement must match it exactly. G-CO.10. Regents scorers check that the last line equals the prove statement; a mismatch — even if all prior steps are correct — earns no credit for that line.

questions.prompt: Statement that has been proved deductively from postulates and earlier results.
questions.explanation: A theorem is a statement established by deductive proof from postulates, definitions, and previously proved theorems. Examples: Triangle Sum Theorem, Pythagorean Theorem, Isosceles Triangle Theorem. Euclid's Elements (~300 BCE) organized Euclidean geometry as a deductive system. G-CO.9. On the Regents, citing a theorem by name (e.g., 'Triangle Sum Theorem') is a valid proof reason.

questions.prompt: Statement accepted as true without proof — also called an axiom.
questions.explanation: A postulate (axiom) is accepted as true without proof and serves as a foundation for all theorems. Examples: Segment Addition Postulate, SAS Postulate, Parallel Postulate. G-CO.9. Euclid listed five postulates in the Elements (~300 BCE); the fifth (Parallel Postulate) states that through a point not on a line, exactly one parallel to that line can be drawn.

questions.prompt: Ordered chain of statements and reasons that moves from given information to the prove statement.
questions.explanation: A logical sequence of statements — each supported by a reason (definition, postulate, theorem, or given) — forms a deductive proof. The two-column format places statements on the left and reasons on the right. G-CO.9. Proofs are graded on logical flow: each statement must follow necessarily from prior lines, and reasons must be precise enough to justify the stated claim.

questions.prompt: Triangle congruence criterion guaranteed when three pairs of corresponding sides are congruent.
questions.explanation: SSS Congruence: if all three pairs of corresponding sides are congruent, the triangles are congruent. Euclid proved SSS in Book I, Proposition 8 of the Elements (~300 BCE). G-CO.8. Mark all three pairs of congruent sides, then state 'SSS' as the reason for the congruence statement. SSS is sufficient — no angle information is needed.

questions.prompt: Triangle congruence criterion guaranteed when two pairs of sides and the included angle are congruent.
questions.explanation: SAS Congruence: two pairs of congruent sides with the included angle congruent between them guarantee triangle congruence. The included angle must be sandwiched between the two named sides — SSA (non-included angle) does NOT guarantee congruence. G-CO.8. A common Regents error is citing SAS when the congruent angle is not between the two congruent sides.

questions.prompt: Triangle congruence criterion guaranteed when two pairs of angles and the included side are congruent.
questions.explanation: ASA Congruence: two pairs of congruent angles with the included side (the side between those two angles) congruent guarantee triangle congruence. G-CO.8. Because two angle pairs being congruent forces the third pair by the Triangle Sum Theorem (180°), ASA and AAS share the same angle information but differ in which side is established as congruent.

questions.prompt: Triangle congruence criterion guaranteed when two pairs of angles and a non-included side are congruent.
questions.explanation: AAS Congruence: two pairs of congruent angles and a non-included (non-between) pair of congruent sides guarantee congruence. Differs from ASA: the congruent side is not between the two angles. G-CO.8. AAS follows from ASA plus the Triangle Sum Theorem — once two angle pairs are equal, the third pair is also congruent, letting us apply ASA on any side.

questions.prompt: Right-triangle congruence criterion using a pair of hypotenuses and one pair of legs.
questions.explanation: HL Congruence applies only to right triangles: if the hypotenuse and one leg are congruent, the triangles are congruent. HL is a special case of SSS — the Pythagorean Theorem determines the third side uniquely. G-CO.8. Always establish the right angles first before citing HL; stating 'HL for right triangles' as the reason is required for full credit.

questions.prompt: Pairs of sides or angles in congruent triangles — the C, P, C, T, C of CPCTC.
questions.explanation: CPCTC: Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles are Congruent. Once triangle congruence is proved (SSS/SAS/ASA/AAS/HL), any pair of corresponding sides or angles may be cited as congruent using CPCTC. G-CO.7. CPCTC is typically the last reason in a proof whose goal is to establish two specific parts — e.g., proving a diagonal bisects a quadrilateral.

questions.prompt: Angle formed between two named sides of a triangle.
questions.explanation: The included angle is formed between two named sides of a triangle — it is the angle at the vertex where those two sides meet. SAS requires the included angle to be between the two congruent sides. G-CO.8. To identify it: find the vertex shared by both named sides. If the angle is not at that shared vertex, it is a non-included angle and SAS does not apply.

questions.prompt: Side that lies between two named angles of a triangle.
questions.explanation: The included side lies between two named angles — it is the segment whose endpoints are the vertices of those two angles. ASA requires the included side to be between the two congruent angles. G-CO.8. To find it: identify the side connecting the two angle vertices. If the side is not between those vertices, it is non-included and AAS applies instead.

questions.prompt: One of the two congruent angles opposite the congruent sides of an isosceles triangle.
questions.explanation: In an isosceles triangle, the base angles are the two congruent angles opposite the congruent sides (legs). The Isosceles Triangle Theorem (G-CO.10) states: if two sides are congruent, the angles opposite them are congruent. Euclid proved this in Book I, Prop. 5 (~300 BCE). Converse: if two angles are congruent, the sides opposite them are congruent.

questions.prompt: Segment from a vertex of a triangle to the midpoint of the opposite side.
questions.explanation: A median connects a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side, creating two congruent segments on that side. A triangle has three medians, concurrent at the centroid — located 2/3 of the way from each vertex to the midpoint. G-CO.10. Medians appear in Regents proofs: if a point is a midpoint, the median creates two congruent halves useful for SSS or SAS.

questions.prompt: Line that crosses two or more other lines, creating angle pairs used in parallel-line proofs.
questions.explanation: A transversal crosses two or more coplanar lines, creating eight angles at two intersections. Regents items name these angles and ask you to apply parallel-line theorems. G-CO.9. Identifying which angle pair is formed (alternate interior, corresponding, co-interior) is the first step; then state the appropriate theorem as the reason for the congruence or supplementary claim.

questions.prompt: Pair of angles on opposite sides of a transversal and between two lines — congruent when the lines are parallel.
questions.explanation: Alternate interior angles lie on opposite sides of the transversal and between the parallel lines. When lines are parallel, alternate interior angles are congruent (Alternate Interior Angles Theorem, G-CO.9). Conversely, if alternate interior angles are congruent, the lines are parallel — a reason usable in proofs to establish that two lines are parallel.

questions.prompt: Pair of angles in matching corners at each intersection of a transversal — congruent when the lines are parallel.
questions.explanation: Corresponding angles occupy matching positions at each intersection of the transversal with the parallel lines (e.g., both upper-left). When lines are parallel, corresponding angles are congruent (Corresponding Angles Postulate, G-CO.9). This postulate is the foundation from which alternate interior and co-interior theorems are proved using supplementary angle reasoning.

questions.prompt: Pair of angles on the same side of a transversal and between two lines — supplementary when the lines are parallel.
questions.explanation: Same-side interior angles (co-interior or consecutive interior angles) are on the same side of the transversal and between the parallel lines. When lines are parallel, they are supplementary (sum to 180°). G-CO.9. Regents proofs often require setting up the equation: ∠1 + ∠2 = 180°. Conversely, if they are supplementary, the lines are parallel.

questions.prompt: Through a point not on a given line, exactly one line can be drawn parallel to the given line.
questions.explanation: Euclid's Parallel Postulate (Book I, Postulate 5, ~300 BCE): through a point not on a given line, exactly one line parallel to that line can be drawn. This postulate is the foundation of Euclidean geometry. G-CO.9. Rejecting it leads to non-Euclidean geometries — a breakthrough of the 19th century. On the Regents, it justifies the existence of unique parallel constructions.

questions.prompt: Quadrilateral proved by showing both pairs of opposite sides are parallel — or both pairs are congruent.
questions.explanation: A parallelogram has both pairs of opposite sides parallel. Proof methods (G-CO.11): show both pairs of opposite sides congruent; show both pairs parallel; show diagonals bisect each other; show one pair of sides both parallel and congruent. On the Regents, the diagonal bisection method is common — prove triangles congruent, then CPCTC the diagonal segments.

questions.prompt: Parallelogram proved by showing it has one right angle or congruent diagonals.
questions.explanation: A rectangle is a parallelogram with four right angles. Proof: show it is a parallelogram with at least one right angle, OR show the diagonals are congruent. G-CO.11. All parallelogram properties apply (opposite sides congruent, opposite angles congruent, diagonals bisect each other) plus: all angles are 90° and diagonals are congruent.

questions.prompt: Parallelogram proved by showing all four sides congruent or perpendicular diagonals.
questions.explanation: A rhombus is a parallelogram with all four sides congruent. Proof methods: show all four sides congruent; show diagonals are perpendicular bisectors of each other; show diagonals bisect the vertex angles. G-CO.11. On Regents proofs, perpendicular diagonals are the most-tested property; use the Pythagorean theorem or slope to verify perpendicularity.

questions.prompt: Quadrilateral that is simultaneously a rectangle and a rhombus.
questions.explanation: A square is both a rectangle and a rhombus — all four sides congruent AND all four angles 90°. Diagonals are congruent, perpendicular, bisect each other, and bisect the vertex angles. G-CO.11. To prove a square: prove it is a rhombus with one right angle, or a rectangle with one pair of congruent adjacent sides. All square properties must hold simultaneously.

questions.prompt: Quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides (the bases).
questions.explanation: A trapezoid has exactly one pair of parallel sides (the bases). An isosceles trapezoid has congruent legs, congruent base angles, and congruent diagonals. G-CO.11. On coordinate Regents proofs, verify: compute slopes to confirm one pair parallel (equal slopes) and one pair not parallel, then use the distance formula to check leg lengths for isosceles.

questions.prompt: Triangle similarity criterion guaranteed when two pairs of corresponding angles are congruent.
questions.explanation: AA Similarity: if two pairs of corresponding angles are congruent, the triangles are similar. Because angles sum to 180° (Triangle Sum Theorem), two congruent angle pairs force the third pair to be congruent as well. G-SRT.3. AA is the most commonly tested similarity criterion on the NY Regents and is the basis of most indirect-measurement problems.

questions.prompt: Triangle similarity criterion using two pairs of proportional sides and a congruent included angle.
questions.explanation: SAS Similarity: two pairs of corresponding sides are proportional AND the included angles are congruent. G-SRT.2. The included angle must be between the two proportional sides — unlike SAS Congruence where sides must be equal, SAS Similarity requires only a constant ratio k. A Regents item may give side lengths; check the ratios and identify the congruent included angle.

questions.prompt: Triangle similarity criterion using three pairs of proportional sides.
questions.explanation: SSS Similarity: all three pairs of corresponding sides are proportional (same ratio k). G-SRT.2. Check by dividing each pair of corresponding sides; if all three ratios are equal, SSS Similarity applies. Unlike SSS Congruence (sides equal), SSS Similarity requires only proportional, not equal, lengths. The common ratio k is the scale factor.

questions.prompt: Triangles with congruent corresponding angles and proportional corresponding sides — written with the ~ symbol.
questions.explanation: Similar triangles have congruent corresponding angles and proportional corresponding sides — all ratios equal the scale factor k. A dilation with factor k maps one onto the other (G-SRT.1). Regents items ask you to write similarity statements (△ABC∼△DEF with correct vertex order) and solve for missing sides by setting up equal ratios from the proportion.

questions.prompt: Diagram in which all lengths are reduced or enlarged from real measurements by the same ratio.
questions.explanation: A scale drawing represents an object with all lengths multiplied by a constant scale factor k. Maps, blueprints, and architectural plans are scale drawings. G-SRT.1. Given the scale ratio (e.g., 1 in = 50 mi), find real distances by multiplying map distances by 50. The scale drawing and the real object form a pair of similar figures with the same ratio throughout.

questions.prompt: Pairs of corresponding sides in similar figures whose ratios are all equal.
questions.explanation: In similar figures, corresponding sides are proportional — their ratios all equal the scale factor k. G-SRT.2. Setting up the proportion (a/b = c/d) and solving is the core computation on Regents similarity items. Always match corresponding sides in the same order in numerator and denominator, using the similarity statement as a guide for which sides correspond.

questions.prompt: Equal product of the means and extremes used to solve a proportion such as a/b = c/d.
questions.explanation: The cross-product property states: if a/b = c/d, then ad = bc (product of means equals product of extremes). G-SRT.5. To solve a Regents proportion: set up the two ratios from corresponding similar sides, cross-multiply, then solve the linear equation. Also called the Cross-Multiplication Property; used extensively in indirect measurement problems.

questions.prompt: Number x such that a/x = x/b — appears in the altitude-on-hypotenuse relationships.
questions.explanation: The geometric mean of a and b is x = √(ab), equivalently a/x = x/b. It appears in the altitude-on-hypotenuse theorem: the altitude from the right angle to the hypotenuse equals the geometric mean of the two hypotenuse segments it creates. G-SRT.4. Example: if segments are 3 and 12, altitude = √(3·12) = 6. Each leg is also a geometric mean.

questions.prompt: Common ratio of corresponding side lengths in two similar figures.
questions.explanation: The similarity ratio (scale factor) k is the constant ratio of any image side to its corresponding preimage side. Perimeters scale by k; areas scale by k². G-SRT.2. Find k by dividing one pair of known corresponding sides. Then use it to find unknown sides (multiply preimage side by k), or verify all three ratios are equal for SSS Similarity.

questions.prompt: Single value k such that y = kx — equal to the similarity ratio for proportional segments.
questions.explanation: When two quantities are proportional (y = kx), k is the constant of proportionality. In similar figures, k equals the similarity ratio — the same constant by which every length scales. G-SRT.2. Recognizing proportional relationships is foundational: if one side doubles, all sides double. This constant ratio is what distinguishes similarity from mere angle congruence.

questions.prompt: Fixed point from which every point of a figure is stretched or shrunk to produce a similar image.
questions.explanation: The center of dilation is the fixed point from which all distances scale by factor k. Coordinate rule with center at the origin: (x,y)→(kx,ky). G-SRT.1a. Lines through corresponding vertices always meet at the center. To find the center algebraically: draw lines through each pair of corresponding vertices and find their intersection point.

questions.prompt: Scale factor k of a dilation — positive value that multiplies every distance from the center.
questions.explanation: The dilation factor k multiplies every distance from the center of dilation. k>1 enlarges; 0<k<1 reduces; k=1 is the identity (no change). G-SRT.1. Area scales by k², so if sides double (k=2), area quadruples. On Regents items: k = image side ÷ preimage side. Finding k from a coordinate diagram requires dividing corresponding coordinate distances.

questions.prompt: Property of dilation — the image of any segment not through the center is parallel to the preimage segment.
questions.explanation: G-SRT.1b: A dilation maps any line segment NOT passing through the center to a parallel segment. This is why parallel lines remain parallel after dilation and why corresponding sides of similar triangles are parallel when one is a dilation of the other. This property is the geometric basis of the Midsegment Theorem and the Side Splitter Theorem.

questions.prompt: Rule (x,y) -> (kx,ky) that produces a dilation centered at the origin with scale factor k.
questions.explanation: The coordinate rule for a dilation centered at the origin with scale factor k is (x,y)→(kx,ky). G-SRT.1. To dilate about a center other than the origin (h,k): subtract (h,k) from each coordinate to shift the center to the origin, apply the rule, then add (h,k) back. Regents items give coordinates and ask for image vertices or the scale factor.

questions.prompt: Scale factor greater than 1 used in a dilation that produces a larger image.
questions.explanation: An enlargement factor k>1 produces a larger similar image with every length multiplied by k and every angle preserved. G-SRT.1. The ratio of areas is k². For example, k=3 means each side is 3× longer and the area is 9× larger. Regents items pair this with perimeter ratio (k:1) and area ratio (k²:1) in multi-part problems requiring both computations.

questions.prompt: Segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half its length.
questions.explanation: The Midsegment Theorem: a segment connecting the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and exactly half its length. G-SRT.4. The midsegment creates a smaller triangle similar to the original with scale factor 1/2. Coordinate proof: use the midpoint formula to find endpoints, then slope to verify parallel, then distance to verify half-length.

questions.prompt: Line parallel to one side of a triangle that intersects the other two sides divides them proportionally.
questions.explanation: The Side Splitter (Triangle Proportionality) Theorem: a line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two sides proportionally. G-SRT.4. If DE∥BC in △ABC, then AD/DB = AE/EC. Converse: if a line divides two sides proportionally, it is parallel to the third side. Both the theorem and its converse appear on Regents constructed-response items.

questions.prompt: Bisector of an angle of a triangle divides the opposite side in the ratio of the two adjacent sides.
questions.explanation: The Angle Bisector Theorem: the bisector of an angle of a triangle divides the opposite side into segments proportional to the two adjacent sides. G-SRT.4. If AD bisects ∠A in △ABC, then BD/DC = AB/AC. Used in Regents constructed-response problems: set up the proportion, cross-multiply, and solve for the unknown segment length.

questions.prompt: Right-triangle segment that creates two smaller triangles similar to the original and to each other.
questions.explanation: When the altitude is drawn from the right angle of a right triangle to the hypotenuse, it creates two smaller triangles each similar to the original and to each other by AA∼. G-SRT.4. Three key proportions result: altitude² = (segment₁)(segment₂); leg² = (full hypotenuse)(adjacent segment). These geometric-mean relationships appear in multi-step Regents problems.

questions.prompt: Ratio of the perimeters of two similar figures, equal to their similarity ratio.
questions.explanation: If two similar figures have similarity ratio k, their perimeters are in ratio k:1 because each side scales by k and the perimeter is the sum of sides. G-SRT.2. Regents multi-part items combine perimeter ratio (k) and area ratio (k²): given one ratio, derive the other. Example: sides in ratio 3:5 → perimeters 3:5, areas 9:25.

questions.prompt: Use of similar triangles to find a length that cannot be measured directly, such as a building's height.
questions.explanation: Indirect measurement uses similar triangles to find a length that cannot be measured directly — e.g., the height of a building or tree. G-SRT.5. Set up two similar triangles (usually via AA∼ from parallel light rays or equal angles), write the proportion of corresponding sides, and solve. This technique was used by ancient surveyors.

questions.prompt: Classic indirect-measurement setup that pairs an object and its shadow with a known stick and its shadow.
questions.explanation: A shadow problem pairs an object (height h, shadow s) with a reference (height H, shadow S): h/s = H/S. G-SRT.5. The sun's parallel rays create congruent angles at the ground, making the triangles similar by AA∼. This method was reportedly used by Thales of Miletus (~600 BCE) to estimate the height of the Great Pyramid from its shadow length.

questions.prompt: Ratio printed on a map such as 1 in : 50 mi that translates map distance to real distance.
questions.explanation: A map scale such as '1 in : 50 mi' is a ratio expressing how map distances relate to real distances. G-SRT.1. Multiply map distance by the scale to get real distance; divide real distance by the scale to get map distance. Map scale is the similarity ratio between the map and reality — all map features are scaled by the same constant factor.

questions.prompt: Ratio that relates a model car, building, or train to its real-world counterpart.
questions.explanation: A model scale expresses the ratio of model size to real size, e.g., 1:24 means 1 inch on the model = 24 inches on the real object. G-SRT.1. Divide a real length by 24 to get model length; multiply a model length by 24 to get the real length. Model scale is a direct application of similarity ratios and appears in Regents multi-step problems.

questions.prompt: Ratio of the areas of two similar figures — equal to the square of the similarity ratio.
questions.explanation: If two similar figures have similarity ratio k, their areas are in ratio k²:1. G-SRT.2. Example: sides in ratio 3:5 → areas in ratio 9:25. Regents items may require finding the side ratio first: k = √(area ratio). Then use k to find missing sides. Volumes of similar 3D figures scale by k³ — an extension tested in G-GMD and solid geometry units.

questions.prompt: Side of a right triangle opposite the right angle — always the longest side.
questions.explanation: The hypotenuse is the side opposite the right angle in a right triangle and is always the longest side. Credited to Pythagoras of Samos (~500 BCE), the relation a²+b²=c² puts c — the hypotenuse — at the center of G-SRT.8. On the Regents, identify the hypotenuse before setting up any trig ratio or applying the Pythagorean theorem.

questions.prompt: Either of the two sides of a right triangle that form the right angle.
questions.explanation: The two legs of a right triangle are the sides that form the right angle. In the Pythagorean theorem a²+b²=c², a and b are the legs. For trig ratios (G-SRT.6), a leg is labeled 'opposite' or 'adjacent' relative to the reference acute angle. Always label the triangle before writing SOH-CAH-TOA to avoid mixing up sides.

questions.prompt: Relationship a^2 + b^2 = c^2 connecting the legs and hypotenuse of a right triangle.
questions.explanation: Attributed to Pythagoras (~500 BCE), the theorem states a²+b²=c² for any right triangle, where c is the hypotenuse. NY standard G-SRT.8 requires applying it to find missing sides. Common Regents setups: find the hypotenuse given two legs, or find a leg given the hypotenuse and one leg. Always check: the largest side must be c.

questions.prompt: Set of three whole numbers, such as 3-4-5 or 5-12-13, that satisfy a^2 + b^2 = c^2.
questions.explanation: A Pythagorean triple is a set of three positive integers satisfying a²+b²=c², such as 3-4-5, 5-12-13, and 8-15-17. Multiples also work: 6-8-10, 9-12-15. Recognizing a triple on the Regents (G-SRT.8) lets you skip the square root and solve faster. Any triple can be scaled by a constant factor to produce another valid triple.

questions.prompt: Angle of exactly 90 degrees — marked with a small square at the vertex.
questions.explanation: A right angle measures exactly 90° and is marked with a small square in diagrams. It defines a right triangle (G-SRT.6-8) and is essential to all trig setups: SOH-CAH-TOA ratios apply only when one angle is 90°. On the Regents, confirm the right-angle marker before applying the Pythagorean theorem or any inverse-trig function.

questions.prompt: Right-triangle trig ratio equal to opposite over hypotenuse.
questions.explanation: Sine is the SOH ratio: sin(θ) = opposite/hypotenuse (G-SRT.6). For a given acute angle θ in a right triangle, the opposite leg is across from θ and the hypotenuse is always the longest side. Use sin(θ) to find a missing opposite side: opposite = hypotenuse × sin(θ). On the Regents, set your calculator to DEGREE mode before evaluating.

questions.prompt: Right-triangle trig ratio equal to adjacent over hypotenuse.
questions.explanation: Cosine is the CAH ratio: cos(θ) = adjacent/hypotenuse (G-SRT.6). The adjacent leg shares the angle θ with the hypotenuse, while the opposite leg is across from it. Use cos(θ) to find missing adjacent sides or the hypotenuse. Remember: in a 60° angle, cos 60° = 0.5 exactly. Verify calculator is in DEGREE mode.

questions.prompt: Right-triangle trig ratio equal to opposite over adjacent.
questions.explanation: Tangent is the TOA ratio: tan(θ) = opposite/adjacent (G-SRT.6). Unlike sine and cosine, tangent does not involve the hypotenuse. Use tan(θ) when the hypotenuse is unknown or irrelevant, such as in angle-of-elevation problems. Note: tan 45° = 1 because opposite = adjacent in an isosceles right triangle. (G-SRT.8)

questions.prompt: Side of a right triangle directly across from the acute angle being referenced.
questions.explanation: In a right triangle, the opposite side is the leg directly across from the reference angle θ. It is the numerator in both the sine ratio (sin = opp/hyp) and the tangent ratio (tan = opp/adj). Correctly identifying opposite vs. adjacent is the most common Regents error in G-SRT.6 questions. Rotate the triangle in your mind: 'opposite' always faces the angle.

questions.prompt: Leg of a right triangle that, together with the hypotenuse, forms the acute angle being referenced.
questions.explanation: The adjacent side is the leg that forms the reference angle θ alongside the hypotenuse — it is not across from θ. It appears in the cosine ratio (cos = adj/hyp) and the tangent denominator (tan = opp/adj). On the Regents (G-SRT.6), students often swap opposite and adjacent; labeling all three sides before computing prevents this error.

questions.prompt: Special right triangle with side ratio 1 : sqrt(3) : 2.
questions.explanation: A 30-60-90 triangle has side ratios 1 : √3 : 2, where 1 is opposite 30°, √3 opposite 60°, and 2 is the hypotenuse (G-SRT.6). These derive from half an equilateral triangle. Regents shortcut: if the short leg is n, the long leg is n√3 and the hypotenuse is 2n. Exact values: sin 30°=½, cos 30°=√3/2, sin 60°=√3/2, cos 60°=½.

questions.prompt: Special right triangle with side ratio 1 : 1 : sqrt(2).
questions.explanation: A 45-45-90 triangle has side ratios 1 : 1 : √2, where both legs are equal and the hypotenuse is leg × √2 (G-SRT.6). This is the isosceles right triangle formed by cutting a square along a diagonal. Regents shortcut: hypotenuse = leg√2; leg = hyp/√2 = hyp√2/2. Exact values: sin 45° = cos 45° = √2/2 ≈ 0.707.

questions.prompt: Exact side length expressed with a square-root symbol, such as 5*sqrt(2), required by Regents free-response.
questions.explanation: A radical side length is an exact answer written with a square-root symbol, such as 5√2 or 3√3, rather than a decimal approximation. NY Regents (G-SRT.8) often ask to 'express in simplest radical form.' To simplify: factor out perfect squares from under the radical (e.g., √48 = 4√3). Leaving the answer in radical form avoids rounding error on multi-step problems.

questions.prompt: Trig value such as sin 60 = sqrt(3)/2 expressed without a calculator decimal.
questions.explanation: Exact trig values are the precise fractions or radicals for special angles: sin 30°=½, cos 45°=√2/2, tan 60°=√3. These come from 30-60-90 and 45-45-90 triangles and are tested on the Regents (G-SRT.6) without a calculator. Memorize the unit-circle row for 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° to answer quickly. Approximate decimal forms are penalized when 'exact' is specified.

questions.prompt: Right triangle with two congruent legs — also called a 45-45-90 triangle.
questions.explanation: An isosceles right triangle has two equal legs and a 90° angle, making the two acute angles both 45°. Side ratio is 1 : 1 : √2 (G-SRT.6). It appears on the Regents when a diagonal of a square is drawn or when a problem states 'two equal legs.' The hypotenuse equals leg × √2; use this to avoid a full Pythagorean-theorem calculation.

questions.prompt: Angle measured upward from a horizontal line of sight to an object above.
questions.explanation: The angle of elevation is measured upward from a horizontal line of sight to an object above the observer. It forms the reference angle θ in a right triangle where the opposite side is the height and the adjacent side is the horizontal distance. NY G-SRT.8 problems often ask: given angle of elevation and horizontal distance, find height using tan(θ) = height/distance.

questions.prompt: Angle measured downward from a horizontal line of sight to an object below.
questions.explanation: The angle of depression is measured downward from a horizontal line of sight to an object below the observer. Because horizontal lines are parallel, the angle of depression equals the angle of elevation from the lower object to the observer (alternate interior angles). G-SRT.8 Regents setup: identify the right triangle, assign opposite/adjacent, then apply SOH-CAH-TOA.

questions.prompt: Imaginary straight line from an observer's eye to the object being viewed.
questions.explanation: The line of sight is the imaginary straight line from an observer's eye to the observed object. In right-triangle trig problems (G-SRT.8), it acts as the hypotenuse of the triangle formed by the observer, the object, and the point directly below the object. Angles of elevation and depression are both measured from horizontal to this line.

questions.prompt: Direction angle measured clockwise from due north, used in navigation problems.
questions.explanation: A bearing is a direction angle measured clockwise from due north, used in navigation. Bearings create right triangles on the coordinate plane (G-SRT.8): convert N 40° E or a three-digit bearing (040°) into a triangle, then apply SOH-CAH-TOA or the Pythagorean theorem to find distances. Draw a compass rose first to set up the correct angle.

questions.prompt: Word problem that asks for a vertical distance using a trig ratio and a measured angle.
questions.explanation: A height problem uses a trig ratio or the Pythagorean theorem to find a vertical measurement such as a building's height or a flagpole (G-SRT.8). Typical setup: measure horizontal distance and angle of elevation, then compute height = distance × tan(angle). Always identify which side is unknown, choose the correct ratio, and check that your calculator is in DEGREE mode.

questions.prompt: Calculator function sin^-1 that returns the angle whose sine is a given ratio.
questions.explanation: The inverse sine function, written sin⁻¹ or arcsin, takes a ratio and returns the angle: θ = sin⁻¹(opp/hyp). Use it when both sides are known but the angle is unknown (G-SRT.7). On the Regents: press 2nd→SIN, enter the ratio, and round to the nearest degree or tenth of a degree as directed. The range of arcsin is −90° to 90°.

questions.prompt: Calculator function cos^-1 that returns the angle whose cosine is a given ratio.
questions.explanation: The inverse cosine function, cos⁻¹ or arccos, returns the angle when the adjacent and hypotenuse are known: θ = cos⁻¹(adj/hyp). G-SRT.7 problems use it when two sides are given and the angle is the unknown. Regents tip: cos⁻¹ produces values between 0° and 180°, so it is appropriate for acute angles in right triangles.

questions.prompt: Calculator function tan^-1 that returns the angle whose tangent is a given ratio.
questions.explanation: The inverse tangent function, tan⁻¹ or arctan, finds the angle when opposite and adjacent sides are known: θ = tan⁻¹(opp/adj). It is the most common inverse-trig function in G-SRT.8 word problems because the hypotenuse is often irrelevant. Regents tip: use 2nd→TAN on a graphing calculator. Range of arctan is −90° to 90°.

questions.prompt: Regents rule such as round to the nearest tenth or nearest degree applied to a trig answer.
questions.explanation: NY Regents instructions specify how to round: 'to the nearest degree,' 'to the nearest tenth,' or 'to the nearest hundredth.' Failing to follow the rounding rule loses a point even if the computation is correct (G-SRT.8). Keep full precision in intermediate steps and round only the final answer. Common error: rounding too early causes a cascade of small errors.

questions.prompt: Setting (DEGREE vs RADIAN) that must match the unit of the angle for trig calculations to be correct.
questions.explanation: A graphing calculator must be set to DEGREE mode for NY Geometry Regents trig problems (G-SRT.6). In RADIAN mode, sin 30° returns ≈0.988 instead of 0.5, causing completely wrong answers. Check: MODE → highlight DEGREE → ENTER. Radian mode is used in pre-calculus and calculus; the Geometry Regents always specifies degrees unless explicitly stated.

questions.prompt: Segment from the center of a circle to any point on the circle.
questions.explanation: The radius is the segment from the center of a circle to any point on the circle (G-C.1). All radii of the same circle are congruent. In the circle equation (x−h)²+(y−k)²=r², the value r is the radius. The radius appears in every circle formula: area = πr², circumference = 2πr, arc length = rθ (radians).

questions.prompt: Chord that passes through the center of a circle — equal in length to twice the radius.
questions.explanation: The diameter is a chord passing through the center, equal to twice the radius: d = 2r (G-C.1). An inscribed angle that intercepts a semicircle (a diameter) always measures 90° — Thales' theorem (G-C.3). On the Regents, if you see an angle inscribed in a semicircle, it is 90°; if you see 90°, the intercepted arc is 180° (a diameter).

questions.prompt: Segment whose endpoints both lie on a circle.
questions.explanation: A chord is a segment whose two endpoints both lie on the circle (G-C.2). The perpendicular from the center to a chord bisects the chord. Two chords that intersect inside a circle satisfy AE·EB = CE·ED (intersecting-chords theorem). The diameter is the longest chord, and equal chords are equidistant from the center.

questions.prompt: Line that intersects a circle at exactly two points.
questions.explanation: A secant is a line (or ray) that intersects a circle at exactly two points (G-C.2). When two secants are drawn from an external point, the angle formed equals half the difference of the intercepted arcs. The external-segment rule: (whole secant) × (external part) = (whole secant) × (external part) for two secants from the same point.

questions.prompt: Line that touches a circle at exactly one point and is perpendicular to the radius at that point.
questions.explanation: A tangent line touches a circle at exactly one point (the point of tangency) and is perpendicular to the radius drawn to that point (G-C.2). Two tangent segments from an external point to the same circle are congruent. The angle formed by a tangent and a chord equals half the intercepted arc. Regents cue: if 'tangent' appears, mark the right angle at the point of tangency immediately.

questions.prompt: Angle whose vertex is at the center of a circle — measure equals the measure of its intercepted arc.
questions.explanation: A central angle has its vertex at the center of the circle and its sides as radii (G-C.2). Its measure equals the measure of its intercepted arc: central angle = arc. This direct relationship makes central angles the anchor for all arc-measure problems. Regents shortcut: arc of a sector = central angle, so sector area = (θ/360)·πr².

questions.prompt: Angle whose vertex lies on a circle — measure equals half its intercepted arc.
questions.explanation: An inscribed angle has its vertex on the circle and its sides as chords (G-C.2, G-C.3). The inscribed angle theorem: inscribed angle = ½ × intercepted arc. Consequence: inscribed angles that intercept the same arc are congruent. Regents rule: if the intercepted arc is 180° (semicircle), the inscribed angle is 90°. This theorem appears on nearly every Geometry Regents circles section.

questions.prompt: Arc of a circle that measures less than 180 degrees.
questions.explanation: A minor arc is the shorter arc between two points on a circle — it measures less than 180° (G-C.2). Named with two letters (e.g., arc AB). Its measure equals the central angle that intercepts it. Inscribed angles and exterior angles that intercept it are half its measure. On the Regents, minor arc problems typically require finding arc measure from a central or inscribed angle.

questions.prompt: Arc of a circle that measures more than 180 degrees.
questions.explanation: A major arc is the longer arc between two points on a circle — it measures more than 180° (G-C.2). Named with three letters to distinguish it from the minor arc (e.g., arc ACB). Its measure = 360° − minor arc. When an exterior angle intercepts two arcs, the angle = ½(major arc − minor arc). Regents problems often test whether students correctly identify which arc to use.

questions.prompt: Arc that measures exactly 180 degrees — endpoints are a diameter.
questions.explanation: A semicircle is an arc measuring exactly 180°, with its endpoints forming a diameter (G-C.2). An inscribed angle intercepting a semicircle is always 90° (Thales' theorem, G-C.3). A semicircle's arc length = πr and its area = πr²/2. On the Regents, when a triangle is inscribed in a circle with one side as a diameter, that triangle is a right triangle.

questions.prompt: Segment from an external point to the point of tangency on a circle.
questions.explanation: A tangent segment connects an external point to the point of tangency (G-C.2). Two tangent segments from the same external point are congruent — this is a Regents staple tested in G-C.2. The tangent is perpendicular to the radius at the point of tangency, forming a right angle useful in Pythagorean-theorem setups. Label the right angle first when drawing the diagram.

questions.prompt: Segment from an external point through a circle, intersecting it at two points.
questions.explanation: A secant segment runs from an external point through a circle and ends at the far intersection (G-C.2). The external part is the portion outside the circle. Secant-secant rule from external point P: (PA)(PB) = (PC)(PD), where A/B and C/D are the two intersection pairs. Regents tip: always identify the 'whole' secant and its 'external' piece before multiplying.

questions.prompt: Pair of chords meeting inside a circle so that the products of their segment lengths are equal.
questions.explanation: When two chords intersect inside a circle at point E, the products of their segments are equal: AE·EB = CE·ED (G-C.2). This 'chord-chord' case of Power of a Point is a frequent Regents question. Set up the equation, cross-multiply, and solve for the unknown segment. Verify by checking that both products match. Draw and label the diagram with all four segment lengths before computing.

questions.prompt: Pair of secants drawn from a common external point — used in the outside-inside product rule.
questions.explanation: Two secant segments drawn from a common external point P satisfy (whole₁)(external₁) = (whole₂)(external₂) (G-C.2). This is the secant-secant case of Power of a Point. Label each segment carefully: 'whole' = external + internal chord, 'external' = portion outside the circle. If one segment is a tangent, the rule becomes: tangent² = whole secant × external part.

questions.prompt: Theorem that the product of segment lengths from a fixed point through a circle is constant regardless of the line chosen.
questions.explanation: Power of a Point is the unified theorem stating that the product of signed distances from an external or interior point to a circle is constant (G-C.2). Three cases: (1) Chord-chord: AE·EB = CE·ED; (2) Secant-secant: PA·PB = PC·PD; (3) Secant-tangent: PT² = PA·PB. Regents problems give three of the four lengths; set up the equation and solve for the fourth.

questions.prompt: Standard form (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 that describes a circle on the coordinate plane.
questions.explanation: The standard form of a circle equation is (x−h)²+(y−k)²=r², where (h,k) is the center and r is the radius (G-GPE.1). Descartes (1637) introduced coordinate geometry, making this algebraic representation possible. On the Regents: read the center and radius directly from standard form; if the equation is in general form, complete the square first.

questions.prompt: Point (h, k) read from a circle equation in standard form.
questions.explanation: In the standard circle equation (x−h)²+(y−k)²=r², the center is the point (h,k) (G-GPE.1). Watch the sign flip: (x−3)²+(y+2)²=25 gives center (3,−2), not (−3,2). Regents tip: the center is the point equidistant from all points on the circle. If the equation is in general form, complete the square to expose h and k.

questions.prompt: Form (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 obtained by completing the square on x and y.
questions.explanation: Standard circle form (x−h)²+(y−k)²=r² makes the center (h,k) and radius r immediately readable (G-GPE.1). Obtained from general form x²+y²+Dx+Ey+F=0 by completing the square on both x and y. Descartes (1637) established this coordinate representation. After completing the square, verify by substituting the center back into the equation.

questions.prompt: Algebraic technique used to rewrite x^2 + y^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0 in standard circle form.
questions.explanation: Completing the square rewrites x²+bx as (x+b/2)²−(b/2)² to convert a circle's general form to standard form (G-GPE.1). Steps: group x-terms and y-terms, add (b/2)² to both sides for each variable, then factor each perfect-square trinomial. This is the primary algebraic skill tested in Unit 6 circle-equation problems on the Regents.

questions.prompt: Length r read directly from a circle equation in the form (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2.
questions.explanation: The coordinate radius r is read directly from the standard circle equation (x−h)²+(y−k)²=r²: take the square root of the right-hand side (G-GPE.1). If the equation shows r²=49, then r=7, not 49. Regents trap: students often forget the square root. Once r is known, area=πr² and circumference=2πr follow immediately.

questions.prompt: Region of a circle bounded by two radii and an arc — equal to (theta/360) * pi * r^2.
questions.explanation: A sector is the 'pie-slice' region bounded by two radii and an arc (G-C.5). Sector area = (central angle / 360°) × πr². For a 90° sector of radius 6: area = (90/360)πr² = (1/4)π(36) = 9π. Regents problems give any two of {angle, radius, area} and ask for the third. Leave answers in terms of π unless told to approximate.

questions.prompt: Length along a circle's edge between two endpoints — equal to (theta/360) * 2 * pi * r.
questions.explanation: Arc length is the distance along the circle's edge between two endpoints (G-C.5). Arc length = (central angle / 360°) × 2πr. Equivalent in radians: arc length = rθ. Example: for a 120° arc on a circle with r=9, arc length = (120/360)×2π(9) = 6π. Arc length and sector area use the same fraction of the full circle.

questions.prompt: Distance around a circle — equal to 2 * pi * r.
questions.explanation: Circumference is the total distance around a circle: C = 2πr = πd (G-C.5, G-GMD.1). It is the arc length of a full 360° arc. Regents use: if circumference is given, divide by 2π to find r before computing area. Exact answers retain π; decimal approximations require rounding to the nearest hundredth per Regents instructions.

questions.prompt: Region enclosed by a circle — equal to pi * r^2.
questions.explanation: The area enclosed by a circle is A = πr² (G-GMD.1). If the diameter is given, compute r = d/2 first. Regents shortcut: area of a semicircle = πr²/2; area of a sector = (θ/360)πr². Leave in terms of π unless the problem says 'nearest tenth' or 'nearest hundredth.' Units are always squared (e.g., cm²).

questions.prompt: Angle measure based on the ratio of arc length to radius — full circle equals 2 * pi radians.
questions.explanation: A radian is defined as the angle subtended by an arc equal in length to the radius (G-C.5). Conversion: 180° = π radians, so 1 rad ≈ 57.3°. Arc length in radians: s = rθ. Sector area: A = ½r²θ. The NY Geometry Regents primarily uses degrees, but radian measure is introduced in G-C.5. Full circle = 2π radians = 360°.

questions.prompt: Formula d = sqrt((x2 - x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2) for the length of a segment on the coordinate plane.
questions.explanation: The distance formula d = √((x₂−x₁)²+(y₂−y₁)²) is the Pythagorean theorem applied to the coordinate plane (G-GPE.7). René Descartes (1637) invented coordinate geometry, making this algebraic measurement possible. Use it to prove side lengths in quadrilateral classification or to verify that a point is on a circle. Always subtract consistently: (x₂−x₁), not (x₁−x₂), though the result is the same when squared.

questions.prompt: Formula ((x1 + x2)/2, (y1 + y2)/2) for the midpoint of a coordinate segment.
questions.explanation: The midpoint formula M = ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2) finds the exact center of a coordinate segment (G-GPE.6). Descartes (1637) enabled this algebraic approach to geometry. On the Regents, use it to: prove diagonals bisect each other (parallelogram proof), find the center of a circle from endpoints of a diameter, or locate a centroid. Average the x-coordinates, average the y-coordinates.

questions.prompt: Rule used to find an unknown endpoint of a segment when its midpoint and the other endpoint are known.
questions.explanation: If the midpoint M=(mx,my) and one endpoint A=(x₁,y₁) are known, the other endpoint is B=(2mx−x₁, 2my−y₁) (G-GPE.6). This rearranges the midpoint formula: mx=(x₁+x₂)/2 → x₂=2mx−x₁. Regents tip: write the midpoint formula, substitute the known values, and solve algebraically. Check: verify that M is exactly halfway between A and your computed B.

questions.prompt: Formula that locates the point dividing a coordinate segment in a given ratio such as 2:3.
questions.explanation: The partition formula locates the point P that divides segment AB in ratio m:n from A: P = (x₁ + m/(m+n)·(x₂−x₁), y₁ + m/(m+n)·(y₂−y₁)) (G-GPE.6). For a 2:3 partition, move 2/5 of the way from A to B. Regents problems state the ratio and ask for the coordinates. The midpoint is the special case m:n = 1:1.

questions.prompt: Two-dimensional grid formed by perpendicular x- and y-axes used to plot points.
questions.explanation: The coordinate plane is the two-dimensional grid formed by perpendicular x- and y-axes intersecting at the origin (0,0). Introduced by René Descartes in La Géométrie (1637), it unites algebra and geometry. Every Coordinate Geometry proof (G-GPE.4) places figures on the coordinate plane so that distances, midpoints, and slopes can be computed algebraically. The four quadrants are labeled I–IV counterclockwise from the upper right.

questions.prompt: Formula m = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) for the slope of a non-vertical line.
questions.explanation: Slope m = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁) measures the steepness of a non-vertical line (G-GPE.5). Positive slope rises left-to-right; negative slope falls. Slope is constant along a line, which is the key fact used in coordinate proofs. Regents use: compute slopes of all four sides of a quadrilateral to test for parallelism or perpendicularity. Order of points does not matter as long as you are consistent.

questions.prompt: Two non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if their slopes are this — equal.
questions.explanation: Two non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if their slopes are equal: m₁ = m₂ (G-GPE.5). Vertical lines (undefined slope) are parallel if they share the same x-intercept — but this case is usually handled separately. On the Regents, prove two sides of a quadrilateral are parallel by computing their slopes and showing they are equal. Equal slopes do not guarantee the same line; check that y-intercepts differ.

questions.prompt: Two non-vertical lines are perpendicular if and only if the product of their slopes is -1.
questions.explanation: Two non-vertical lines are perpendicular if and only if their slopes are negative reciprocals: m₁ × m₂ = −1 (G-GPE.5). Example: slopes of 3/4 and −4/3 are perpendicular. A vertical line (undefined slope) is perpendicular to a horizontal line (zero slope). On the Regents, use this to prove right angles in coordinate proofs, identify altitudes, or write the equation of a perpendicular bisector.

questions.prompt: Slope of a vertical line — denominator x2 - x1 equals zero.
questions.explanation: A vertical line has undefined slope because the denominator x₂−x₁ = 0 in the slope formula, making the fraction undefined (G-GPE.5). Its equation is x = k for some constant k. A vertical line is perpendicular to any horizontal line. On the Regents, if two points share the same x-coordinate, the line through them is vertical and perpendicular to the x-axis.

questions.prompt: Slope of a horizontal line — numerator y2 - y1 equals zero.
questions.explanation: A horizontal line has slope 0 because y₂−y₁ = 0 in the slope formula (G-GPE.5). Its equation is y = k for some constant k. A horizontal line is parallel to the x-axis and perpendicular to any vertical line. On the Regents, two points with the same y-coordinate define a horizontal segment with slope zero, often used to identify a base in a coordinate proof.

questions.prompt: Proof that uses coordinates and algebraic formulas to verify a geometric property.
questions.explanation: A coordinate proof places a figure on the coordinate plane and uses the distance, midpoint, or slope formula to verify a property (G-GPE.4). Descartes (1637) made this algebraic approach possible. Typical Regents task: given a quadrilateral's vertices, prove it is a parallelogram (equal slopes), rectangle (perpendicular slopes), or rhombus (equal sides). State the property you proved in a conclusion sentence.

questions.prompt: Result of a coordinate proof that shows a quadrilateral is a parallelogram, rectangle, rhombus, or square based on side and diagonal evidence.
questions.explanation: In a coordinate proof (G-GPE.4), a quadrilateral is classified by its side lengths and slopes. Parallelogram: two pairs of parallel sides. Rectangle: parallelogram + 90° angles. Rhombus: parallelogram + all sides equal. Square: rectangle + rhombus. Compute all four slopes and all four side lengths, then match to the definition. The Regents often asks you to 'justify your answer' — state which property you used.

questions.prompt: Coordinate-proof evidence such as the diagonals bisect each other or are perpendicular.
questions.explanation: In a coordinate proof (G-GPE.4), diagonal properties confirm a quadrilateral's classification. Parallelogram: diagonals bisect each other (midpoints are equal). Rectangle: diagonals are congruent (equal distances). Rhombus: diagonals are perpendicular (slopes are negative reciprocals). Checking diagonals is often faster than checking all four sides. Compute both midpoints and/or both diagonal lengths to argue the property.

questions.prompt: Use of the distance formula on each side of a quadrilateral to argue for a specific classification.
questions.explanation: Computing side lengths with the distance formula d=√((x₂−x₁)²+(y₂−y₁)²) provides algebraic evidence in coordinate proofs (G-GPE.4, G-GPE.7). Equal opposite sides → parallelogram. All four sides equal → rhombus. Adjacent sides equal → kite. Regents tip: compute in order AB, BC, CD, DA to avoid mistakes. Leave answers in simplest radical form unless the problem asks for a decimal.

questions.prompt: Use of slopes of opposite or adjacent sides to argue parallelism or perpendicularity in a coordinate proof.
questions.explanation: Slope evidence in a coordinate proof (G-GPE.4, G-GPE.5) uses the slope formula m=(y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁) to argue parallelism (equal slopes) or perpendicularity (negative reciprocal slopes). Compute slopes of all four sides, then compare opposite pairs for parallelism and adjacent pairs for perpendicularity. State the conclusion: 'Since opposite sides have equal slopes, AB ∥ CD and BC ∥ DA, so ABCD is a parallelogram.'

questions.prompt: Equation whose graph is a line — commonly written y = mx + b.
questions.explanation: A linear equation in two variables produces a straight-line graph; in slope-intercept form y = mx + b, m is slope and b is y-intercept (G-GPE.5). Descartes (1637) unified algebra and geometry so lines could be expressed as equations. On the Regents, convert between slope-intercept, point-slope, and standard forms as needed. Two distinct parallel lines have the same m but different b.

questions.prompt: Equation y - y1 = m(x - x1) used to write a line through a given point with a given slope.
questions.explanation: Point-slope form y−y₁ = m(x−x₁) writes the equation of a line through a given point (x₁,y₁) with slope m (G-GPE.5). It is the fastest form when slope and one point are known. Regents uses: write the equation of the perpendicular bisector (find midpoint → find perp. slope → apply point-slope). Convert to slope-intercept by distributing m and adding y₁ to both sides.

questions.prompt: Line through the midpoint of a segment that is perpendicular to it — the locus of points equidistant from the endpoints.
questions.explanation: The perpendicular bisector of a segment passes through its midpoint and is perpendicular to it (G-CO.1, G-GPE.5). Steps: find midpoint M using the midpoint formula; find slope of the segment; take the negative reciprocal for the bisector's slope; write equation in point-slope form through M. Every point on the perpendicular bisector is equidistant from the segment's two endpoints — the locus definition.

questions.prompt: Segment from a vertex of a triangle to the midpoint of the opposite side, drawn on the coordinate plane.
questions.explanation: A median of a triangle is the segment from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side (G-GPE.4). To find it in a coordinate proof: use the midpoint formula on the opposite side, then write the equation of the line through the vertex and that midpoint. The three medians of a triangle meet at the centroid, which is located 2/3 of the way from each vertex to the opposite midpoint.

questions.prompt: Segment from a vertex of a triangle perpendicular to the line containing the opposite side.
questions.explanation: An altitude of a triangle is the segment from a vertex perpendicular to the opposite side (or its extension) (G-GPE.4). To find it in a coordinate proof: find the slope of the opposite side; take the negative reciprocal; write the line through the vertex with that slope. The three altitudes meet at the orthocenter. Regents tip: verify perpendicularity by confirming m₁ × m₂ = −1.

questions.prompt: Set of all points that satisfy a given geometric condition.
questions.explanation: A locus is the set of all points satisfying a given geometric condition (G-CO.1). Common loci in the NY Geometry Regents: all points equidistant from a point → circle; equidistant from two points → perpendicular bisector; equidistant from two parallel lines → a parallel line midway between them; equidistant from two intersecting lines → the angle bisectors.

questions.prompt: Points whose distances to two fixed objects are equal — locus is the perpendicular bisector or angle bisector.
questions.explanation: Points equidistant from two fixed objects are equally far from each. Equidistant from two points → perpendicular bisector of the segment joining them (G-CO.1). Equidistant from two lines → angle bisector(s) (G-CO.1). Regents locus problems require identifying which locus applies, then describing or drawing it. Use the distance formula or perpendicular-bisector construction to verify equidistance.

questions.prompt: Locus of points equidistant from two fixed points — a line perpendicular to the segment at its midpoint.
questions.explanation: All points equidistant from two fixed points A and B lie on the perpendicular bisector of segment AB (G-CO.1, G-GPE.5). To find it: compute midpoint of AB; compute slope of AB; take the negative reciprocal slope; write the equation through the midpoint. Regents tip: this locus is a full line, not just a segment. It is also the locus equidistant from the two endpoints.

questions.prompt: Locus of points a fixed distance from a single point — a circle of that radius.
questions.explanation: All points a fixed distance r from a single fixed point P form a circle with center P and radius r (G-CO.1, G-GPE.1). This is the formal definition of a circle. On the Regents, a locus question may describe 'all points 5 units from (2,−3)' — translate directly to the circle equation (x−2)²+(y+3)²=25. The circle equation is the algebraic representation of this locus.

questions.prompt: Solution to a compound locus problem — the points satisfying two conditions at once.
questions.explanation: In a compound locus problem, the solution is the set of points satisfying two conditions simultaneously — the intersection of the two individual loci (G-CO.1). Method: draw each locus separately; count and identify where they overlap. Example: locus equidistant from two points (perpendicular bisector) intersected with locus a fixed distance from a third point (circle) may yield 0, 1, or 2 points. State the number and coordinates of intersections.

questions.prompt: Region of a triangle — equal to (1/2) * base * height.
questions.explanation: A triangle's area equals ½ × base × height. This formula derives from the fact that any triangle is half a parallelogram (G-GMD.A). On the NY Regents, triangles often appear inside composite figures — identify the correct base-height pair (they must be perpendicular) before calculating. A 3-4-5 right triangle has area ½(3)(4) = 6 sq units.

questions.prompt: Region of a parallelogram — equal to base * height.
questions.explanation: A parallelogram's area equals base × height, where height is perpendicular to the base — not the slant side. NY CC G-GMD.A. Since a parallelogram can be cut and rearranged into a rectangle with the same base and height, the formula mirrors rectangle area. Regents items often give a side length and an angle; use trigonometry to find the perpendicular height first.

questions.prompt: Region of a trapezoid — equal to (1/2)(b1 + b2) * height.
questions.explanation: A trapezoid's area equals ½(b₁ + b₂) × h, where b₁ and b₂ are the two parallel sides and h is the perpendicular distance between them. NY CC G-GMD.A. The formula averages the two bases then multiplies by height — equivalent to treating the trapezoid as a triangle with base (b₁ + b₂). Regents prompts frequently embed the trapezoid inside a coordinate grid.

questions.prompt: Region of a regular polygon — equal to (1/2) * apothem * perimeter.
questions.explanation: A regular polygon's area equals ½ × apothem × perimeter (G-GMD.A). The apothem is the perpendicular from the center to any side. For a regular hexagon with side s, the apothem is s√3/2. Regents items may give a polygon's side length and ask you to compute the apothem using 30-60-90 or tangent ratios before finding total area.

questions.prompt: Region of a figure built from simpler shapes whose individual areas are added or subtracted.
questions.explanation: Composite area splits an irregular figure into rectangles, triangles, trapezoids, semicircles, or other standard shapes, then adds or subtracts (G-GMD.A). On the NY Regents, a common task is finding the area of a room with a cutout, or a shaded region between two shapes. Carefully identify which pieces to add and which to subtract to avoid sign errors.

questions.prompt: Sum of the areas of two congruent polygonal bases and rectangular lateral faces.
questions.explanation: A prism's surface area equals 2 × (base area) + lateral area, where the lateral area is the perimeter of the base times the height (G-GMD.A). For a rectangular prism with l × w × h, SA = 2(lw + lh + wh). Regents items often ask for the amount of material needed to cover a box — always check whether the problem wants all faces or only the lateral surface.

questions.prompt: Total area 2 * pi * r^2 + 2 * pi * r * h of two circular bases and a rectangular lateral surface.
questions.explanation: A closed cylinder's surface area is 2πr² + 2πrh — two circular bases plus the lateral rectangle whose width equals the circumference 2πr (G-GMD.A). The NY Regents reference sheet includes this formula. Common errors: forgetting a base, or confusing diameter with radius. For an open cylinder (like a can without a lid), subtract one πr² term.

questions.prompt: Sum of one polygonal base area and the areas of triangular lateral faces.
questions.explanation: A pyramid's surface area equals the base area plus the sum of the triangular lateral face areas. Each lateral face area is ½ × base edge × slant height (G-GMD.A). The slant height is NOT the pyramid's height — use the Pythagorean Theorem: slant height = √(height² + (½ base)²). The NY Regents frequently tests the slant-height calculation as a separate step.

questions.prompt: Total area pi * r^2 + pi * r * l of a circular base and a curved lateral surface.
questions.explanation: A cone's total surface area is πr² + πrl, where r is the base radius and l is the slant height (G-GMD.A). The lateral part πrl comes from unrolling the cone into a sector. Slant height l = √(r² + h²). The NY Regents reference sheet lists this formula. Problems often give the cone's height, requiring a Pythagorean step before computing SA.

questions.prompt: Curved surface measure 4 * pi * r^2.
questions.explanation: A sphere's surface area equals 4πr² (G-GMD.A). Archimedes (c. 225 BCE) proved this equals the lateral surface of the circumscribed cylinder. On the NY Regents reference sheet it appears as SA = 4πr². A common application: a baseball or soccer ball problem asks for the amount of leather covering — substitute the given radius and leave the answer in terms of π or rounded.

questions.prompt: Capacity of a prism — equal to base area * height.
questions.explanation: A prism's volume equals (base area) × height, V = Bh (G-GMD.A.3). This follows from Cavalieri's principle (1635): the solid can be stacked as layers each equal to the base. For a rectangular prism V = lwh. The NY Regents reference sheet lists Bh. Watch for oblique prisms — height is still perpendicular, not the lateral edge.

questions.prompt: Capacity pi * r^2 * h of a circular cylinder.
questions.explanation: A cylinder's volume is V = πr²h — the circular base area πr² stacked h units high (G-GMD.A.3). Cavalieri's principle (1635) shows an oblique cylinder has the same volume as a right cylinder of equal r and h. The formula is on the NY Regents reference sheet. A common item gives diameter, so halve it: r = d/2 before squaring.

questions.prompt: Capacity (1/3) * base area * height of a pyramid.
questions.explanation: A pyramid's volume is V = ⅓ × B × h, where B is the base area (G-GMD.A.3). Three congruent pyramids fill a prism of the same base and height, giving the ⅓ factor. The NY Regents reference sheet lists this formula. For a square pyramid with base side s and height h: V = ⅓s²h. Problems often require finding h using the Pythagorean Theorem first.

questions.prompt: Capacity (1/3) * pi * r^2 * h of a circular cone.
questions.explanation: A cone's volume is V = ⅓πr²h — one-third of the cylinder with the same base and height (G-GMD.A.3). Cavalieri's principle confirms this for oblique cones. The NY Regents reference sheet includes this formula. A classic item: water fills a conical cup of radius 3 cm and height 9 cm — V = ⅓π(9)(9) = 27π ≈ 84.8 cm³.

questions.prompt: Capacity (4/3) * pi * r^3 of a sphere.
questions.explanation: A sphere's volume is V = (4/3)πr³ (G-GMD.A.3). Archimedes (c. 225 BCE) proved it equals two-thirds the volume of the circumscribed cylinder. The NY Regents reference sheet lists this formula. A frequent item: find how many spherical shot-put balls (r = 6 cm) are cast from a rectangular block of iron — divide block volume by (4/3)π(6³).

questions.prompt: Square of the similarity ratio — relates the areas of two similar figures.
questions.explanation: When two figures are similar with linear scale factor k, their areas scale by k² (G-SRT.A). If a small triangle has area 12 cm² and k = 3, the large triangle's area is 12 × 9 = 108 cm². NY Regents items often give one area and ask for the other — identify k from a pair of corresponding lengths first, square it, then multiply or divide the given area.

questions.prompt: Cube of the similarity ratio — relates the volumes of two similar solids.
questions.explanation: When two solids are similar with linear scale factor k, their volumes scale by k³ (G-GMD.A).
