If a small cone has volume 10 in³ and k = 2, the large cone's volume is 10 × 8 = 80 in³. NY Regents items pair a volume question with a surface-area question on the same similar solids; use k² for area and k³ for volume.

questions.prompt: Similarity ratio k that relates corresponding lengths of two similar figures.
questions.explanation: The linear scale factor k is the ratio of any pair of corresponding lengths in two similar figures (G-SRT.A). For a photo enlarged from 4 in to 10 in on one side, k = 10/4 = 2.5. All distances, perimeters, and segment lengths scale by k; areas scale by k²; volumes by k³. On the Regents, identify k before answering any scale-factor question.

questions.prompt: Ratio of mass to volume — a Regents modeling quantity such as grams per cubic centimeter.
questions.explanation: Density = mass ÷ volume (G-MG.A.2). NY CC standard G-MG.A.2 specifically calls for using density to solve design problems. Units are typically g/cm³ or lb/ft³. A Regents item might give an aluminum sphere (density 2.7 g/cm³) and ask for its mass: first find V = (4/3)πr³, then mass = density × V. Keeping units consistent prevents common errors.

questions.prompt: Process of changing measurements between units, such as cubic inches to cubic feet, in a volume problem.
questions.explanation: Unit conversion in volume problems requires cubing the linear conversion factor (G-GMD.A). Since 1 ft = 12 in, 1 ft³ = 1728 in³. A tank measured in inches must be converted to feet before reporting volume in ft³. On the NY Regents, dimensional analysis — writing units as fractions and canceling — is the safest approach for multi-step conversion problems.

questions.prompt: Two-dimensional figure formed when a plane intersects a three-dimensional solid.
questions.explanation: A cross section is the 2-D figure formed when a plane cuts through a 3-D solid (G-GMD.B.4). The shape depends on both the solid and the angle of the cut. NY CC G-GMD.B.4 asks students to identify cross sections of prisms, pyramids, cylinders, and cones. Regents items present a diagram and ask which 2-D shape results — visualizing the intersection is the key skill.

questions.prompt: Cross section taken by a plane parallel to a chosen base — congruent for prisms and cylinders.
questions.explanation: Cutting a prism or cylinder with a plane parallel to its base produces a cross section congruent to that base (G-GMD.B.4). For a rectangular prism, every horizontal slice is a congruent rectangle. For a cylinder, every horizontal slice is a congruent circle. This constancy at every height is exactly why Cavalieri's principle (1635) guarantees V = Bh for these solids.

questions.prompt: Cross section taken by a plane perpendicular to a chosen axis — vertical slice of a solid.
questions.explanation: A plane perpendicular to a cylinder's axis produces a circular cross section; perpendicular to a rectangular prism's length gives a rectangular cross section (G-GMD.B.4). For a cone, a perpendicular cut through the apex gives a triangle; through the base gives a circle. NY Regents items ask students to match the solid and cut direction to the correct 2-D shape.

questions.prompt: Three-dimensional solid generated by rotating a two-dimensional shape about an axis.
questions.explanation: Rotating a 2-D shape around an axis generates a 3-D solid of revolution (G-GMD.B.4). A rectangle rotated about one side makes a cylinder; a right triangle rotated about a leg makes a cone; a semicircle rotated about its diameter makes a sphere. NY CC G-GMD.B.4 includes this rotation concept. Regents items may describe the rotation and ask students to name or sketch the resulting solid.

questions.prompt: Principle that two solids with the same height and equal-area cross sections at every level have equal volumes.
questions.explanation: Cavalieri's principle (Bonaventura Cavalieri, 1635): if two solids have equal heights and equal cross-sectional areas at every level, their volumes are equal (G-GMD.A.1). This justifies using V = πr²h for oblique cylinders and V = ⅓Bh for oblique cones. The NY Regents expects students to cite or apply this principle when explaining why a volume formula holds for non-right solids.

questions.prompt: Property such as right angle, parallel, or congruent that may or may not be assumed from the look of a figure.
questions.explanation: In a geometric diagram, only information explicitly marked — tick marks for equal lengths, square corners for right angles, arrows for parallel — may be assumed (G-MG.A.1). Appearances can be misleading: angles that look right may not be. On the NY Regents, students lose points for assuming properties from diagrams without stated evidence. Always cite the label or given.

questions.prompt: Physical or mathematical limit, such as a budget or material amount, that bounds a modeling problem.
questions.explanation: A constraint is a physical or mathematical limit that bounds a modeling problem (G-MG.A.3). Examples: a fence of exactly 120 ft, a budget of $500, or a container that must hold at least 1 L. NY CC G-MG.A.3 expects students to identify constraints and use them to set up equations or inequalities. Ignoring a constraint leads to answers outside the feasible range.

questions.prompt: Degree of detail of a measurement — limited by the smallest unit the instrument can read.
questions.explanation: Precision describes the fineness of a measurement — limited by the smallest unit the instrument can read (G-MG.A.3). A ruler marked in millimeters gives measurements precise to ±0.5 mm. On the Regents, computed answers should be rounded to the precision the problem specifies ('nearest tenth,' 'nearest cubic foot'). Reporting more digits than warranted implies false precision.

questions.prompt: Allowable range of variation in a measurement, such as plus or minus 0.5 cm.
questions.explanation: Measurement tolerance is the allowable range of variation around a target value, such as 5.0 cm ± 0.5 cm (G-MG.A.3). In engineering, a part that falls outside tolerance is rejected. On the NY Regents, a modeling problem may ask whether a manufactured cylinder meets a volume specification given a radius measured with a stated tolerance — substitute the extreme values to check.

questions.prompt: Estimate that is close enough for a real-world purpose without claiming false precision.
questions.explanation: A reasonable approximation is close enough for a real-world purpose without claiming false precision (G-MG.A.3). Modeling a basketball as a perfect sphere, or a window as a rectangle, simplifies calculation while preserving useful accuracy. NY Regents constructed-response items award credit for sensible approximations accompanied by correct formula use and appropriate rounding.

questions.prompt: Drawing made with only a compass and straightedge — no measured ruler or protractor.
questions.explanation: A compass-and-straightedge construction uses only a compass (for equal distances) and an unmarked straightedge (for straight lines) — no rulers or protractors (G-CO.D.12). The straightedge draws lines; the compass copies lengths and strikes arcs. Constructions model the logical foundations of Euclidean geometry. NY Regents items ask students to describe or sketch steps for standard constructions.

questions.prompt: Drawing made using an unmarked straightedge together with a compass.
questions.explanation: A straightedge-and-compass construction is the classical Euclidean tool set: an unmarked edge plus a compass (G-CO.D.12). Together they can bisect angles, copy segments, erect perpendiculars, and inscribe regular polygons. The Regents expects students to construct figures and explain each step with a geometric justification. No measurements are allowed in a pure construction.

questions.prompt: Ray that divides an angle into two congruent angles — constructed with compass arcs from the vertex.
questions.explanation: To bisect an angle with a compass: place the compass at the vertex and draw an arc crossing both sides; then from each intersection draw equal arcs that meet inside the angle; connect the vertex to that meeting point (G-CO.D.12). The bisector divides the angle into two congruent angles. NY Regents items may ask students to draw the construction or justify why each arc-step guarantees congruence.

questions.prompt: Line that cuts a segment in half at a right angle — constructed with arcs from each endpoint.
questions.explanation: To construct a perpendicular bisector: open the compass to more than half the segment's length; draw arcs above and below from each endpoint; connect the two intersection points (G-CO.D.12). The resulting line is perpendicular to the segment and passes through its midpoint. The Regents tests this construction directly and uses it as a step in circumscribing a circle about a triangle.

questions.prompt: Compass-and-straightedge construction that reproduces a given angle at a new vertex.
questions.explanation: To copy an angle at a new vertex: draw a ray from the new vertex; place the compass at the original vertex and strike an arc crossing both sides; copy that arc at the new vertex; measure the chord between the two original crossings and mark the same chord from the arc at the new vertex; connect to the new vertex (G-CO.D.12). The new angle is congruent to the original.

questions.prompt: Three-dimensional representation in which every length is the same fraction of the real object.
questions.explanation: A scale model is a three-dimensional object in which every length is the same fixed fraction of the real object (G-MG.A.1). A 1:50 architectural model means 1 cm on the model equals 50 cm in reality. Volume scales by 50³ = 125,000. NY Regents G-MG items ask students to convert between model and real dimensions using the scale ratio, or to find real volumes from model measurements.

questions.prompt: Scaled top-down drawing of a building's rooms, walls, and doorways.
questions.explanation: A floor plan is a scaled top-down drawing showing a building's rooms, walls, doors, and windows (G-MG.A.1). Architects state the scale (e.g., ¼ in = 1 ft). NY Regents problems provide a floor plan with a scale bar and ask for the real area of a room or the total flooring cost. Students multiply measured lengths by the scale factor and then apply area formulas.

questions.prompt: Triangle drawn on the ground by a surveyor whose measured sides and angles fix locations of distant points.
questions.explanation: Surveyors establish a triangle on the ground — measuring two sides and the included angle, or all three sides — to fix the location of an inaccessible point using the Law of Sines or Cosines (G-SRT.D.11, G-MG.A.1). NY Regents items give a surveying scenario with a scale diagram and ask students to compute a distance, typically using the sine rule or the Pythagorean Theorem.

questions.prompt: Compass direction such as N 30 E used to describe a path or location.
questions.explanation: A bearing is a compass direction such as N 30° E (read as '30 degrees east of north') used to describe navigation paths (G-MG.A.1). Bearing problems on the NY Regents typically draw a diagram, label angles from north, and ask for distance or direction using trigonometry. A bearing of due east is 090°; south is 180°. Sketch the diagram before writing equations.

questions.prompt: Detailed scale drawing that engineers and architects use to communicate a design.
questions.explanation: A design blueprint is a detailed scale drawing that communicates exact dimensions, materials, and tolerances to engineers and builders (G-MG.A.1). Unlike a sketch, a blueprint uses standardized symbols, precise scale, and labeled dimensions. NY Regents G-MG problems present blueprint-style figures and ask students to compute areas, volumes, or material quantities using stated dimensions and scale.

questions.prompt: Shortest possible length connecting two or more points subject to a constraint.
questions.explanation: Finding the minimum distance between two points subject to a constraint often involves reflecting one point across a boundary line and drawing a straight path (G-MG.A.3). The shortest path from A to a wall to B equals the straight-line distance from A to the reflection of B. NY Regents items frame this as a 'lifeguard problem' or 'pipeline problem' requiring a reflection or calculus-free algebra.

questions.prompt: Largest possible area achievable for a given perimeter or set of materials.
questions.explanation: For a fixed perimeter P, the shape that encloses maximum area is a square (equal sides) among rectangles, or a circle among all shapes (G-MG.A.3). On NY Regents modeling tasks, students are given a fixed amount of fencing and asked which rectangular dimensions maximize area. Setting up A = l × w with the constraint 2l + 2w = P and solving algebraically (or by symmetry) gives the answer.

questions.prompt: Route of least total length between two points, sometimes routed through a reflection or fixed point.
questions.explanation: The shortest path between two points that must touch a line (mirror, wall, or river) is found by reflecting one endpoint across the line and connecting with a straight segment (G-MG.A.3). This reflection principle appears in lifeguard, billiard, and pipeline routing problems on the NY Regents. The straight-line distance after reflection equals the minimum total path length.

questions.prompt: Limit on the amount of fencing, sheet metal, or wrapping paper available — drives an optimization decision.
questions.explanation: A material constraint caps the total area of sheet metal, fabric, or fencing available, forcing a trade-off between dimensions (G-MG.A.3). In a box problem, fixing surface area and maximizing volume leads to a specific relationship between length, width, and height. On the Regents, students set up one equation from the constraint and a second formula for the quantity being optimized.

questions.prompt: Geometric model of a box, can, or other container designed to minimize material or maximize content.
questions.explanation: A packaging model uses geometric solids — cylinders, rectangular prisms, cones — to minimize material (surface area) for a fixed volume, or maximize volume for a fixed surface area (G-MG.A.3). A classic Regents scenario: design a cylindrical can holding 500 cm³ with the least tin — set V = πr²h = 500, express SA in terms of r, and compare candidate dimensions. The optimal can has height ≈ 2r.

questions.prompt: Reference such as 'by the Pythagorean Theorem' that supports a step in a modeling explanation.
questions.explanation: A theorem citation names the specific rule supporting a step: 'by the Pythagorean Theorem,' 'by SAS congruence,' or 'by Cavalieri's principle' (G-MG.A.3, G-CO.B). On the NY Regents constructed-response section, a geometric justification that lacks a named theorem earns partial credit at best. The citation connects a calculation to the logical framework that makes it valid.

questions.prompt: Numerical work shown to support a modeling claim, including units and rounding.
questions.explanation: Calculation evidence shows the numerical work that supports a geometric modeling claim, including correct units and rounding (G-MG.A.3). On a NY Regents constructed-response, a student who states 'the area is 48' without showing computation earns zero. The Regents rubric rewards each step: set up the formula, substitute values, compute, and state units with the final answer.

questions.prompt: Single case used to disprove a general claim by showing it fails.
questions.explanation: A counterexample disproves a universal claim by exhibiting one specific case where the claim fails (G-CO.B). If a student claims 'all quadrilaterals with perpendicular diagonals are squares,' a rhombus with unequal sides serves as a counterexample. On the NY Regents, a two-point question may ask students to disprove a given statement — one valid counterexample earns full credit.

questions.prompt: Final statement that ties the calculations and theorems back to the modeling question.
questions.explanation: The proof conclusion ties all calculations and cited theorems back to the original modeling question (G-MG.A.3). On the NY Regents, a conclusion such as 'therefore the sphere fits inside the box because 904 cm³ < 1000 cm³' earns the final credit point. Omitting the conclusion — even after correct work — loses the point. Write a sentence that directly answers the question asked.

questions.prompt: Logical chain of statements in which the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises.
questions.explanation: A valid argument is a logical chain where the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises (G-CO.B). In geometry proofs, each statement must be supported by a reason — given, definition, postulate, or previously proved theorem. On the NY Regents, an argument that skips a step or uses an unjustified assumption is invalid and earns partial credit. Every logical gap must be filled.

questions.prompt: Argument that two figures are congruent because a sequence of rigid motions maps one onto the other.
questions.explanation: A rigid-motion proof shows that a sequence of reflections, rotations, or translations maps one figure exactly onto another, proving congruence (G-CO.B.8). On the NY Regents, students describe each transformation by name and parameters (e.g., 'reflect over the y-axis, then rotate 90° about the origin'). The image must coincide with the target — every point must map correctly.

questions.prompt: Angle relationship — alternate interior, corresponding, or co-interior — established by a transversal crossing parallel lines.
questions.explanation: When a transversal crosses parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent, corresponding angles are congruent, and co-interior (same-side interior) angles are supplementary (G-CO.C.9). These relationships appear in nearly every NY Regents proof involving parallel lines. Cite the specific pair ('alternate interior angles with parallel lines AB ∥ CD') rather than just 'parallel lines.'

questions.prompt: Decision such as parallelogram, rectangle, rhombus, or square based on side and diagonal evidence.
questions.explanation: Quadrilaterals are classified by their sides and diagonals: a parallelogram has opposite sides parallel; a rectangle has four right angles; a rhombus has four equal sides; a square has both (G-CO.C.11). On the NY Regents, coordinate-geometry proofs use slope (for parallel/perpendicular) and distance (for equal lengths) to determine which special quadrilateral a set of four points forms.

questions.prompt: Reason cited at each step of a two-column proof — a given, definition, postulate, or theorem.
questions.explanation: Each step in a two-column proof needs a reason: 'Given,' a definition (e.g., 'definition of midpoint'), a postulate (e.g., 'Segment Addition Postulate'), or a previously proved theorem (e.g., 'Isosceles Triangle Theorem') (G-CO.B). On the NY Regents, missing reasons cost points even when statements are correct. Memorize the common reason names for triangle congruence and angle relationships.

questions.prompt: Equation such as a/b = c/d set up from corresponding sides of similar triangles.
questions.explanation: Similar triangles produce proportions: corresponding sides are in equal ratio. Set up a/b = c/d where a, c are one triangle's sides and b, d are the matching sides of the other (G-SRT.B.5). Cross-multiply to solve for an unknown. On the NY Regents, the proportion often arises from AA similarity — two triangles share an angle and both contain a right angle.

questions.prompt: Step in which the hypotenuse, opposite, and adjacent sides are labeled before applying a trig ratio.
questions.explanation: Before applying a trig ratio, label the right triangle's three sides relative to the reference angle: the hypotenuse (opposite the right angle), the opposite side (across from the reference angle), and the adjacent side (next to the reference angle) (G-SRT.C.6). Mislabeling — especially confusing opposite and adjacent — is the most common error in Regents trig items.

questions.prompt: Decision among sine, cosine, and tangent based on which two sides the problem involves.
questions.explanation: Choose sine when you know or want opposite and hypotenuse; cosine for adjacent and hypotenuse; tangent for opposite and adjacent (G-SRT.C.6). SOH-CAH-TOA is the memory aid. On the NY Regents, read the problem to find which two of the three sides are involved, then pick the matching ratio. For missing angles, use the inverse function: θ = sin⁻¹(opp/hyp).

questions.prompt: Use of similar triangles or trig ratios to find a length that cannot be measured directly.
questions.explanation: Indirect measurement uses similar triangles or trig ratios to find a length that cannot be measured directly — the height of a building, the width of a river (G-SRT.B.5, G-SRT.C.8). A classic Regents setup: a surveyor stands 80 ft from a tree and measures a 32° angle of elevation to the top; height = 80 × tan(32°) ≈ 50 ft. Always draw and label the diagram first.

questions.prompt: Theorem that an inscribed angle equals half its intercepted arc.
questions.explanation: An inscribed angle equals half its intercepted arc (G-C.A.2). If the arc measures 110°, the inscribed angle is 55°. A corollary: all inscribed angles that intercept the same arc are congruent. On the NY Regents, inscribed-angle problems often involve a circle with several chords — identify which arc each angle intercepts before calculating.

questions.prompt: Property that a tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the radius drawn to the point of tangency.
questions.explanation: A tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the radius drawn to the point of tangency (G-C.A.2). This creates a right angle, enabling Pythagorean Theorem use: if r = 5 and the external segment = 12, the tangent length = √(12² − 5²) = √119. On the Regents, tangent-radius problems often ask for a missing length in a diagram where the radius and external distance are given.

questions.prompt: Process of writing (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 from a center and a radius, or from a general second-degree equation.
questions.explanation: The standard circle equation is (x − h)² + (y − k)² = r², where (h, k) is the center and r is the radius (G-GPE.A.1). To convert a general equation like x² + y² + 4x − 6y = 3, complete the square for both x and y: (x+2)² + (y−3)² = 16 → center (−2, 3), r = 4. NY Regents items frequently ask for center and radius from a general-form equation.

questions.prompt: Coordinate-geometry argument that uses both slope and distance formulas to classify a polygon.
questions.explanation: A slope-distance coordinate proof classifies a polygon by computing slopes (to check parallel/perpendicular sides) and distances (to check equal lengths) (G-GPE.B.4). For a parallelogram proof: show opposite sides have equal slope AND equal length. For a rectangle: also show adjacent sides have slopes that are negative reciprocals. Label each pair of sides clearly.

questions.prompt: Set of points that satisfy two locus conditions simultaneously, often the answer to a compound construction problem.
questions.explanation: A locus is a set of points satisfying a condition (G-GPE.B). Two locus conditions intersect at the points satisfying both simultaneously. For example, points equidistant from two given points lie on their perpendicular bisector; points 3 cm from a fixed point lie on a circle. Their intersection satisfies both. Regents items describe loci verbally and ask students to sketch, count, and name intersection points.

questions.prompt: Reference-sheet formula such as 2 * pi * r * h + 2 * pi * r^2 for a closed cylinder.
questions.explanation: Surface-area formulas appear on the NY Regents reference sheet: cylinder SA = 2πr² + 2πrh; cone SA = πr² + πrl; sphere SA = 4πr²; lateral SA of a prism = perimeter × height (G-GMD.A). Use the reference sheet — do not memorize formulas under test pressure. The most common error is forgetting a base or confusing slant height with height in cone problems.

questions.prompt: Reference-sheet formula such as (1/3) * pi * r^2 * h for a cone.
questions.explanation: Volume formulas on the NY Regents reference sheet: prism/cylinder V = Bh; pyramid/cone V = ⅓Bh; sphere V = (4/3)πr³ (G-GMD.A.3). Cavalieri's principle (1635) justifies applying Bh to oblique prisms and cylinders. Always identify B (base area), h (perpendicular height), and r before substituting. Leave answers in terms of π unless the problem says to round.

questions.prompt: Idea that lengths scale by k, areas by k^2, and volumes by k^3 under a dilation with factor k.
questions.explanation: Under a dilation with scale factor k: all lengths scale by k, all areas scale by k², all volumes scale by k³ (G-SRT.A, G-GMD.A). If two similar cylinders have radii in ratio 2:5, their surface areas are in ratio 4:25 and their volumes in ratio 8:125. On the Regents, identify k from a pair of corresponding lengths, then apply the correct power for the quantity requested.

questions.prompt: Two- or three-dimensional figure built from simpler shapes whose measures must be combined.
questions.explanation: A composite figure combines multiple standard shapes — rectangles, triangles, semicircles, prisms, cylinders — whose individual measures must be combined by addition or subtraction (G-GMD.A). On the Regents, break the composite into identifiable parts, compute each part's area or volume using reference-sheet formulas, then add (or subtract for cutouts). Label each part before computing.

questions.prompt: Step that converts between units of length, area, or volume to keep an answer consistent.
questions.explanation: Unit conversion in geometry uses the fact that linear factors cube for volume: 1 ft = 12 in, so 1 ft³ = 1728 in³ (G-GMD.A). A pool measured in feet must be converted to gallons using 1 ft³ ≈ 7.48 gal. On the Regents, dimensional analysis — writing units as fractions and canceling — prevents errors. Write out the full conversion chain before multiplying.

questions.prompt: Specific measurement or marking read from the figure that supports a claim on a Regents constructed response.
questions.explanation: On a Regents constructed-response, diagram evidence is a specific measurement or label from the figure that supports the claim — not a general statement (G-MG.A.3). For example: 'The diagram shows AC = 8 cm and BC = 15 cm, so by the Pythagorean Theorem AB = 17 cm.' Vague references like 'the diagram shows it' earn zero. Cite the exact value and where it appears.

questions.prompt: Choice of the correct named theorem that justifies a step in a Regents explanation.
questions.explanation: A theorem match pairs the situation in the problem with the correct named theorem (G-CO, G-SRT, G-C). Saying 'angles are equal' without naming the theorem earns partial credit; 'alternate interior angles are congruent because AB ∥ CD' earns full credit. On the Regents, common theorems to match: Pythagorean Theorem, inscribed angle theorem, SAS similarity, Cavalieri's principle.

questions.prompt: Equation or substitution shown numerically to back up a geometric claim.
questions.explanation: Algebraic support on a Regents response shows the equation or substitution that backs up a geometric claim, with all values labeled (G-MG.A.3). Example: 'V = (4/3)π(6)³ = 288π ≈ 904.8 cm³, which is less than 1000 cm³, so the sphere fits.' Stating a conclusion without the algebra earns zero for that credit point. Show every substitution and simplification step.

questions.prompt: Sentence such as 'rounded to the nearest tenth' required by the Regents directions on a numerical answer.
questions.explanation: When a Regents problem directs 'round to the nearest tenth' or 'express your answer to the nearest whole number,' the rounding statement is a required part of the response (G-MG.A.3). An exact or unrounded answer earns partial credit. Write the rounded value and include the unit: '≈ 47.1 ft².' Omitting the rounding statement when required costs the final credit point.

questions.prompt: Disproof of a general claim by exhibiting one specific case where it fails.
questions.explanation: A counterexample disproves a universal claim by showing one specific case where it fails (G-CO.B). On the NY Regents, a two-point task might state 'all parallelograms are rectangles — agree or disagree and justify.' One counterexample (e.g., a rhombus with a 60° angle) earns full credit for the disagree response. The counterexample must be specific with labeled measurements or coordinates.
